page: UpCloud CLOUDNATIVE-24xCPU-256GB is a KVM-based virtual server featuring 24 vCPUs and 256.0 GB of RAM. The system is powered by an AMD EPYC 7542 processor operating at 2.0 GHz on the x86_64 architecture. It provides 24 physical cores with a shared CPU allocation model, yielding 10.67 GB of memory per core. No local storage or dedicated GPU hardware is bundled with this instance.

In benchmark evaluations, the server demonstrates average multi-core performance, as evidenced by its stress-ng multi-core and Geekbench multi-core scores. Single-core performance is relatively weak, with stress-ng single-core results falling in the bottom 10%. However, multi-threaded bzip3 decompression performance is strong, placing in the top 25%. Memory bandwidth benchmarks for both read and write operations remain in the average tier, though the Passmark memory score is positioned in the bottom 25%.

Given the high memory-to-vCPU ratio of over 10 GB per core, this server offers a resource-dense profile suitable for memory-intensive workloads. The shared CPU allocation model presents a tradeoff, making it less ideal for sustained, high-performance compute tasks but highly cost-efficient for memory-heavy applications that do not require continuous peak CPU performance. Recommended workloads include large-scale databases, caching layers such as Redis, memory-intensive data processing, and general-purpose applications requiring substantial memory capacity.

Powered by an AMD EPYC 7542 processor running at 2.0 GHz on the x86_64 architecture, this instance utilizes a shared CPU allocation model. It does not include local storage or GPU accelerators. Performance benchmarks indicate average multi-core capabilities, highlighted by strong multi-threaded bzip3 decompression, though single-core performance and memory scores are relatively weak. This resource profile makes the instance a cost-effective option for memory-intensive workloads that do not demand sustained, dedicated CPU performance. It is well-suited for database operations, caching layers, and memory-heavy data processing tasks.
